followups.a: Brokers are Raft observers that consume the `__cluster_metadata` log as a stream of events and apply them to a local cache, rather than the old model where a single controller watched ZooKeeper and pushed RPCs to each broker. Replaying an event log is faster and more consistent, and a restarting broker just resumes the log from its last applied offset instead of re-fetching full state.

followups.q: What does migrating an existing ZooKeeper cluster to KRaft involve, and by which version is it mandatory?
followups.a: There's a dual-write migration: you stand up a KRaft controller quorum, put the cluster in migration mode so metadata is written to both ZooKeeper and KRaft, migrate brokers to KRaft mode, then finalize and decommission ZooKeeper. ZooKeeper support was removed entirely in Kafka 4.0, so any cluster upgrading to 4.x must already be on KRaft.

q: With the idempotent producer, what does max.in.flight.requests.per.connection control, and how does it relate to ordering and throughput?
a: max.in.flight.requests.per.connection is how many unacknowledged produce requests the client allows in flight per broker connection before it blocks. Historically, retries with more than one in-flight request could reorder messages, so people set it to 1 to preserve order at the cost of throughput. The idempotent producer fixes this: the broker tracks producer ID plus per-partition sequence numbers and rejects out-of-order or duplicate batches, so Kafka guarantees both ordering and dedup-on-retry as long as max.in.flight is at most 5. Enabling idempotence (default in 3.0+) auto-sets acks=all, retries effectively to max, and caps in-flight at 5 — giving you ordered, exactly-once-per-session delivery without dropping to a single in-flight request.
fj: To keep messages ordered I have to set max.in.flight to 1.
fs: Only without idempotence. The idempotent producer (default 3.0+) preserves order and dedups retries with up to 5 in-flight requests via producer-id + sequence numbers — so I keep throughput and ordering.

followups.q: Why is the idempotent producer's safe in-flight limit exactly 5, not unlimited?
followups.a: The broker tracks the last 5 batches' sequence numbers per producer-id per partition to detect duplicates and out-of-order arrivals. With more than 5 in flight, a retried earlier batch could fall outside that window and be misordered or re-accepted, so Kafka caps `max.in.flight` at 5 when idempotence is on. It's the largest window that still guarantees ordering-plus-dedup on retry.

followups.q: How exactly does the broker use producer ID and sequence number to reject a duplicate retry?
followups.a: On `init`, the producer gets a producer ID (PID); each batch to a partition carries a monotonically increasing sequence number per PID. The broker remembers the last sequence it accepted per PID-partition. A retried batch arrives with a sequence it has already seen, so the broker acknowledges it as a no-op duplicate; a gap in the sequence triggers an OutOfOrderSequenceException.

followups.q: Does enabling idempotence preserve ordering across a producer restart, and why or why not?
followups.a: No. The PID is assigned per producer session, so on restart the producer gets a new PID and the broker's per-PID sequence tracking resets — duplicates or reordering across the restart boundary aren't deduplicated. Cross-session exactly-once and ordering require the transactional API with a stable `transactional.id`, whose epoch fencing carries identity across restarts.

q: What is Kafka tiered storage (KIP-405), and what problem does it solve?
a: Traditionally a partition's entire log lives on local broker disk, so long retention forces big, expensive disks and makes brokers slow to rebalance because moving a replica copies all its data. Tiered storage splits the log into a local tier (recent segments on broker disk) and a remote tier (older closed segments offloaded to object storage like S3/GCS), controlled by local.retention.ms/bytes for local and the normal retention.ms/bytes for total. This lets you keep hours locally for hot reads while retaining months or years cheaply in object storage, and it shrinks broker disk so failover and reassignment move far less data. It was early-access in 3.6 and has matured since; the trade-off is that reads of cold historical data fetch from remote storage with higher latency, so it suits append-and-occasionally-replay workloads.
fj: Long retention in Kafka just means buying bigger broker disks.
fs: Tiered storage (KIP-405) offloads old segments to S3/GCS while keeping recent data local — cheap long retention and faster rebalancing, at the cost of higher-latency reads for cold historical data.

followups.q: Why does tiered storage make broker rebalancing and replica reassignment much faster?
followups.a: Only the local tier (recent segments) sits on broker disk; the bulk of the log lives in object storage shared across brokers. When you reassign a replica or replace a broker, Kafka copies just the small local tier instead of the entire partition history, so data movement during rebalances drops by orders of magnitude — shrinking the window of reduced redundancy.

followups.q: What is the latency trade-off when a consumer reads data that has been offloaded to the remote tier?
followups.a: Reads of recent data come from local disk/page cache at normal speed, but a consumer seeking into cold segments triggers a fetch from object storage (S3/GCS), which has much higher per-read latency and request cost. So tiered storage suits append-and-occasionally-replay workloads; a consumer constantly streaming from far behind the head pays remote-read latency continuously.

followups.q: How do local.retention.ms and retention.ms interact to decide what's local versus remote versus deleted?
followups.a: `local.retention.ms`/`.bytes` bound how much recent data stays on broker disk; once a closed segment exceeds that, it's offloaded to the remote tier but still readable. The overall `retention.ms`/`.bytes` bounds total lifetime — when a segment passes that, it's deleted from remote storage entirely. So local retention governs the hot/cold boundary, total retention governs final expiry.

q: What is rack awareness in Kafka, and how does follower fetching reduce cross-AZ cost?
a: Setting broker.rack (where a rack is any failure domain — a physical rack, an availability zone, a region) makes Kafka spread a partition's replicas across different racks so a whole-rack/AZ outage cannot take out every copy. By default consumers always read from the partition leader, which in a multi-AZ cluster often means cross-AZ network traffic and real cloud egress cost. Follower fetching fixes this: set the broker replica.selector.class to RackAwareReplicaSelector and set client.rack on consumers, and a consumer will fetch from an in-sync replica in its own rack/AZ instead of the (possibly remote) leader. This cuts cross-AZ data-transfer charges and network load, at the cost of slightly staler reads since a follower may lag the leader within the ISR bound.
fj: Rack awareness is only about surviving a rack failure.
fs: Replica placement is one half; the other is follower fetching — with RackAwareReplicaSelector + client.rack, consumers read from a same-AZ in-sync replica instead of the leader, cutting cross-AZ egress cost.

code.label: Enable follower fetching
lines: # now reads stay in-AZ -> less egress $

followups.q: What is the staleness trade-off of follower fetching, and when is it unacceptable?
followups.a: A consumer reading from a same-AZ follower sees data only up to that follower's fetch position, which can lag the leader by up to `replica.lag.time.max.ms` while still being in the ISR — so reads are slightly staler. That's fine for analytics and most streaming, but for a read-after-write or strict freshness path you keep reading from the leader and accept the cross-AZ cost.

followups.q: Does writing still go cross-AZ even with follower fetching enabled?
followups.a: Yes. Follower fetching only redirects consumer reads to a local replica; producers always write to the partition leader, which may be in another AZ, and the leader still replicates to followers across AZs. So follower fetching cuts consumer-egress cross-AZ traffic but doesn't eliminate the produce and replication cross-AZ paths.

followups.q: How does setting broker.rack actually influence where Kafka places partition replicas?
followups.a: Kafka's replica assignment is rack-aware: it spreads the leader and followers of each partition across distinct `broker.rack` values so no single rack/AZ holds all copies. That guarantees a whole-rack or whole-AZ outage leaves at least one in-sync replica alive. Without `broker.rack` set, the placement algorithm can unluckily land multiple replicas in the same failure domain.

q: What are Schema Registry subject naming strategies, and why would you move off the default TopicNameStrategy?
a: The Schema Registry versions schemas under a subject, and the subject naming strategy decides what that subject is. The default TopicNameStrategy uses <topic>-key and <topic>-value, which assumes one schema type per topic and enforces compatibility per topic — simple and correct for most pipelines. RecordNameStrategy uses the record's fully-qualified name as the subject, so the same schema evolves consistently across many topics and a single topic can carry multiple event types. TopicRecordNameStrategy combines both (<topic>-<recordName>), giving multiple event types per topic while still scoping compatibility per topic. You move off the default mainly to put heterogeneous event types on one topic (e.g. an event-sourcing or outbox stream) while keeping each type's compatibility enforced independently.
fj: Each topic can only ever hold one schema, so the subject is always just the topic.
fs: That's only TopicNameStrategy. RecordNameStrategy / TopicRecordNameStrategy key the subject on the record type, letting one topic carry multiple event types with per-type compatibility — useful for event-sourcing/outbox streams.

followups.q: What problem does RecordNameStrategy introduce that TopicNameStrategy avoids?
followups.a: RecordNameStrategy scopes the subject (and thus compatibility) to the record's fully-qualified name across all topics, so the same type evolves consistently everywhere — but a breaking change to that type now affects every topic carrying it, widening the blast radius. TopicRecordNameStrategy narrows it back to per-topic-per-type, trading the cross-topic consistency for tighter, independent evolution scopes.

followups.q: When you put multiple event types on one topic, how does a consumer know which schema to deserialize each record with?
followups.a: It still uses the wire-format header: every record carries its own 4-byte schema ID regardless of subject strategy, so the deserializer fetches the exact schema for that record by ID. The subject strategy only governs how compatibility is grouped at registration time — it doesn't change per-record deserialization, which is always ID-driven.

followups.q: Why is putting heterogeneous event types on one topic useful for event sourcing or an outbox stream?
followups.a: Event sourcing needs all events for an aggregate in strict order on one partition, but those events are different types (Created, Updated, Deleted). TopicNameStrategy would force them into one merged schema; RecordName/TopicRecordName strategy lets each type keep its own schema and compatibility while sharing the topic and key, preserving per-aggregate ordering across mixed event types.
